examples.title: Interval Scheduling Greedy Algorithm
examples.description: Selects maximum number of non-overlapping intervals using greedy approach
examples.code:
ALGORITHM IntervalScheduling(intervals)
BEGIN
  // intervals is array of (start, finish) pairs
  n ← length(intervals)
  
  // Step 1: Sort intervals by finish time
  Sort intervals by finish time in ascending order
  
  // Step 2: Greedy selection
  result ← empty list
  last_finish_time ← -∞
  
  FOR i ← 1 TO n DO
    IF intervals[i].start ≥ last_finish_time THEN
      result.append(intervals[i])
      last_finish_time ← intervals[i].finish
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  RETURN result
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm works in two phases: first sorting intervals by their finish times in O(n log n), then making a single pass selecting intervals that don't conflict with previously selected ones. The key insight is that choosing the interval that finishes earliest leaves maximum space for subsequent intervals.

examples.title: Interval Scheduling Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation of interval scheduling greedy algorithm
examples.code:
def interval_scheduling(intervals):
    '''
    intervals: list of tuples (start, finish)
    Returns: list of selected intervals
    '''
    if not intervals:
        return []
    
    # Sort by finish time
    sorted_intervals = sorted(intervals, key=lambda x: x[1])
    
    # Greedy selection
    result = []
    last_finish = float('-inf')
    
    for start, finish in sorted_intervals:
        if start >= last_finish:
            result.append((start, finish))
            last_finish = finish
    
    return result

# Example usage
intervals = [(1, 4), (2, 3), (3, 6), (4, 5), (5, 7), (6, 8)]
selected = interval_scheduling(intervals)
print("Selected intervals:", selected)  # Output: [(2, 3), (4, 5), (5, 7)]
examples.explanation: The Python implementation follows the pseudocode exactly. The sorted() function uses the finish time as the sorting key. The greedy selection iterates once through sorted intervals, adding an interval only if it starts after or at the last finish time.

examples.title: Fractional Knapsack Greedy Algorithm
examples.description: Solves fractional knapsack by selecting items based on value-to-weight ratio
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FractionalKnapsack(items, capacity)
BEGIN
  // items is array of (value, weight) pairs
  n ← length(items)
  
  // Step 1: Calculate value/weight ratio for each item
  FOR i ← 1 TO n DO
    items[i].ratio ← items[i].value / items[i].weight
  END FOR
  
  // Step 2: Sort items by ratio in descending order
  Sort items by ratio in descending order
  
  // Step 3: Greedy selection
  total_value ← 0
  remaining_capacity ← capacity
  
  FOR i ← 1 TO n DO
    IF remaining_capacity = 0 THEN
      BREAK
    END IF
    
    IF items[i].weight ≤ remaining_capacity THEN
      // Take entire item
      total_value ← total_value + items[i].value
      remaining_capacity ← remaining_capacity - items[i].weight
    ELSE
      // Take fraction of item
      fraction ← remaining_capacity / items[i].weight
      total_value ← total_value + (items[i].value * fraction)
      remaining_capacity ← 0
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  RETURN total_value
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm first computes and sorts items by their value-to-weight ratio in descending order. Then it greedily selects items, taking whole items until the remaining capacity is less than the next item's weight, in which case it takes a fractional part to fill the knapsack completely.

examples.title: Fractional Knapsack Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation with both value maximization and item selection
examples.code:
def fractional_knapsack(items, capacity):
    '''
    items: list of tuples (value, weight)
    capacity: maximum weight knapsack can hold
    Returns: (max_value, list of (value, weight, fraction taken))
    '''
    # Calculate ratio and sort by descending ratio
    item_ratios = []
    for value, weight in items:
        ratio = value / weight if weight > 0 else 0
        item_ratios.append((value, weight, ratio))
    
    # Sort by ratio descending
    item_ratios.sort(key=lambda x: x[2], reverse=True)
    
    total_value = 0
    remaining = capacity
    selected = []
    
    for value, weight, ratio in item_ratios:
        if remaining == 0:
            break
        
        if weight <= remaining:
            # Take whole item
            total_value += value
            remaining -= weight
            selected.append((value, weight, 1.0))
        else:
            # Take fraction
            fraction = remaining / weight
            total_value += value * fraction
            selected.append((value, weight, fraction))
            remaining = 0
    
    return total_value, selected

# Example
items = [(60, 10), (100, 20), (120, 30)]
capacity = 50
max_val, selected = fractional_knapsack(items, capacity)
print(f"Maximum value: {max_val}")  # Output: 240.0
print("Selected items with fractions:", selected)
examples.explanation: The implementation returns both the maximum value and details of what was selected including fractions. For the example with items (60,10), (100,20), (120,30) and capacity 50, it selects all of first two items and 2/3 of the third, giving total value 240.
